finder_output.summary: Both documents define open finance as a framework for permissioned/consent-driven sharing of customer financial information between data holders and third parties.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our framework mandates standardised, machine-readable API-based data transmission, consistent with BIS's finding that standardised APIs are the backbone of interoperable open finance ecosystems.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: We adopt a regulatory-mandate/prescriptive approach with binding standards and mandated FSPs, matching BIS's regulation-led prescriptive category.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our framework emphasises financial inclusion as a goal, aligning with BIS's finding that inclusion is a top motivation for open finance frameworks.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: We include single account view and credit underwriting use cases that BIS identifies as key open finance applications driving adoption and credit access.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both stress security, cyber risk management and secure API/data transmission controls as foundational to open finance.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: We prescribe a detailed, granular consent regime (voluntary, revocable, time-limited, dashboard, deletion on expiry), which BIS treats only as a high-level building block without prescribing specifics.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: We set binding requirements for third-party service provider handling of customer information and secure disposal, which BIS does not address in prescriptive detail.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: We phase in obligations gradually via a multi-year timeline, consistent with BIS's observation that jurisdictions typically implement open finance in phases starting from open banking.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: We specify board and senior management oversight obligations for open finance, an governance dimension BIS does not cover.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: BIS discusses cross-border data portability and interoperability challenges (Project Aperta), which our domestic-focused policy does not address.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: BIS raises data access pricing/fees as an emerging policy issue, which our framework is silent on.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: BIS notes payment initiation/write access (e.g. PSD2) drives faster adoption, whereas our framework is read-only information sharing without payment initiation.
finder_output.label: differs-on

finder_output.summary: BIS highlights low adoption and behavioural/awareness barriers, particularly for underserved groups; we address this only through optional guidance on inclusive interface design.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: Our optional guidance encourages inclusive, accessible interface design for underserved segments, partially responsive to BIS's evidence on lower adoption among disadvantaged groups.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: Both documents define open finance as a framework for permissioned/consent-driven sharing of customer financial information between data holders and third parties.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Our framework mandates standardised, machine-readable API-based data transmission, consistent with BIS's finding that standardised APIs are the backbone of interoperable open finance ecosystems.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We adopt a regulatory-mandate/prescriptive approach with binding standards and mandated FSPs, matching BIS's regulation-led prescriptive category.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: BNM binding standards ('S') apply to mandated FSPs per Appendix 1; BIS notes mandatory participation is not proven a necessary condition for a level playing field.

critic_output.summary: Our framework emphasises financial inclusion as a goal, aligning with BIS's finding that inclusion is a top motivation for open finance frameworks.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We include single account view and credit underwriting use cases that BIS identifies as key open finance applications driving adoption and credit access.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Both stress security, cyber risk management and secure API/data transmission controls as foundational to open finance.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We prescribe a detailed, granular consent regime (voluntary, revocable, time-limited, dashboard, deletion on expiry), which BIS treats only as a high-level building block without prescribing specifics.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond
critic_output.scope_note: Revocation right is subject to exception where disclosure is necessary to comply with legal or contractual requirements; recurring consent capped at six months.

critic_output.summary: We set binding requirements for third-party service provider handling of customer information and secure disposal, which BIS does not address in prescriptive detail.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond
critic_output.scope_note: Dropped invented anchors BNM 11.9 and BNM 11.10, which do not exist in the provided anchor list.

critic_output.summary: We phase in obligations gradually via a multi-year timeline, consistent with BIS's observation that jurisdictions typically implement open finance in phases starting from open banking.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We specify board and senior management oversight obligations for open finance, a governance dimension BIS does not cover in prescriptive detail.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: BIS discusses cross-border data portability and interoperability challenges (Project Aperta), which our framework references only aspirationally as a national/regional Open Data ambition without operational requirements.
critic_output.label: silent-on
critic_output.scope_note: BNM 1.6 aspires to regional data-sharing initiatives aligned with global standards but sets no cross-border portability rules.

critic_output.summary: BIS raises data access pricing/fees as an emerging policy issue, which our framework does not address.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: BIS notes payment initiation/write access (e.g. PSD2) drives faster adoption, whereas our framework is read-only information sharing without payment initiation.
critic_output.label: differs-on

critic_output.summary: BIS highlights low adoption and behavioural/awareness barriers, particularly for underserved groups; our framework addresses this only through optional guidance on inclusive interface design.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: Our optional guidance encourages inclusive, accessible interface design for underserved segments (including persons with disabilities and low digital literacy), responsive to BIS's evidence on lower adoption among disadvantaged groups.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond
critic_output.scope_note: Corrected target from BIS Pap168 6 (which concerns risks generally) to BIS Pap168 15 on adoption barriers; note this is only 'G' guidance, not a binding standard.

critic_output.summary: We require data consumers to apply safeguards to received customer information at least equivalent to those for their own information and prohibit sharing with third parties without explicit consent — a data protection reciprocity dimension BIS does not prescribe.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: We mandate a customer breach handling and response plan with escalation and remedial procedures for open finance data breaches, which BIS identifies as a risk area but does not prescribe controls for.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: BIS frames open finance largely as a competition/anti-data-monopoly remedy; our framework centres on secure, responsible data sharing and risk management balanced against innovation rather than competition policy per se.
critic_output.label: differs-on

critic_output.summary: BIS discusses centralised vs decentralised technical architectures as a jurisdictional design choice; our framework adopts a platform-operator/centralised platform model without articulating this as an explicit architectural policy choice.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: We prohibit bundled/blanket consent and require voluntary, non-coerced consent that cannot be a precondition for a product/service — a consumer-protection safeguard BIS notes only generally under governance/consent layers.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond
